nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes and lagoons in turbid coastal environments; generally uncommon in the wild

decorPreferences: lower rockwork or sand bed placement
open space away from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Weekly target feeding of micro-plankton, zooplankton, or small frozen foods (such as brine shrimp or copepods) is generally advised to support long-term health and growth.

goodWith: clownfish (may host in absence of anemone)
avoidWith: aggressive sweeper-tentacle corals placed in close proximity
fish known to nip at coral polyps
hammer or torch corals without adequate spacing

summary: Alveopora is an LPS coral with 12-tentacled flower-like polyps, closely related to but generally hardier than Goniopora. Moderate care; prefers low flow and moderate light near the sand bed.
careNotes:
Alveopora is a genus of approximately 17 recognized species in the family Acroporidae (order Scleractinia). Despite its soft, flowing appearance, it builds a hard calcium carbonate skeleton with a notably light and porous structure composed of interconnecting rods and spines. Polyps are large and fleshy with swollen knob-like tentacle tips, and colonies may be massive or branching.

Identification vs. Goniopora: Alveopora and Goniopora are frequently confused in the aquarium trade due to their very similar appearance — both are sometimes called 'Flowerpot Coral.' The key morphological distinction is tentacle count: Alveopora polyps have 12 tentacles per polyp, while Goniopora polyps have 24. Alveopora is also in family Acroporidae, whereas Goniopora belongs to family Poritidae. Importantly, Alveopora is generally considered hardier and more forgiving in captivity than Goniopora, making it a more accessible option for intermediate reef keepers.

Placement and flow: Generally advised to place near the bottom of the aquarium, on or near the sand bed, in low, indirect water movement. Direct laminar flow tends to cause polyp retraction. Polyps will extend dramatically when healthy and conditions are favorable.

Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally recommended. Alveopora has been observed to display a striking glowing effect under actinic (blue) lighting. Gradual light acclimation is advised when introducing new specimens.

Water chemistry: Regular supplementation with calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium is important for skeletal growth. Standard reef parameters of calcium 380–450 ppm, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, and magnesium 1250–1450 ppm are commonly recommended, consistent with other LPS corals. Strontium and trace elements have also been noted as beneficial.

Aggression: Alveopora is not considered an aggressive coral, but fully extended polyps can reach and sting neighboring corals. Maintaining adequate spacing from neighbors (at least 10–15 cm) is advised.

Clownfish association: Clownfish have been observed hosting in Alveopora colonies in the absence of an anemone, though this is not obligate and the coral generally tolerates it.

Cross-source note: BRS did not carry an Alveopora product; WWC product pages returned 404 during this research session. Primary data sourced from LiveAquaria (two confirmed product pages with identical parameters). Taxonomy and biological identification confirmed via Wikipedia.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Alveopora Coral (second listing)
additional.notes: Cross-check listing; confirmed identical water parameters (temp 72-78°F, SG 1.023-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-12), care level, flow (low), and placement (bottom). Both LiveAquaria listings agree fully.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Alveopora
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Acroporidae, order Scleractinia), native range (Indo-Pacific reef slopes and turbid environments), species count (~17 species), and biological identification: 12 tentacles per polyp with swollen knob-like tips distinguishes Alveopora from Goniopora (24 tentacles). Not used for husbandry parameters.
